Now, any further ado, I want to officially announce the acquisition ClickFunnels is just acquired Magnetic Marketing, Dan Kennedy's company. I'm so excited for a couple of reasons. Number one, for me as like probably Dan's biggest fan, I'm so honored to have a chance to be able to take my mentors work and be able to take this and pass on to the next generation of marketers. I was 20 years old when I ran into this stuff and it changed my life forever. I want to be able to bring that to the next generation of entrepreneurs and help change their life as well. That's number one. Number two, importantly, is for you guys. I want to be able to take these principles from Dan and give them to you so you have a more holistic understanding of business. Right now, all the things that people are teaching on the internet are these tactics. The things that are here that work today but are gone tomorrow, the fleeting things. The reason why I've been in business for over two decades now is because my business was not built on these fly-by-night tactics. It's because I built it on a solid foundation on strategies, the best marketers in the world. So I'm going to be giving you guys these strategies from Dan that will help grow your e-con business for you to be able to take your coaching businesses and grow them and scale them at levels you never thought were possible. Dan changed my life and he's going to change your life as well. And I'm excited to tell you that your training begins right now. Thank you. Thank you. There's Dan Kennedy, you guys. Thank you. So it's nice of them all to be so excited because Frank Kern is still thoroughly pissed off that we ruined his story and that I ruined his story by not dying. He clung to the idea that I was dead for quite some time. We were sending him pictures of me holding up newspapers with the current date on him to try and persuade him that I was still around. So thank you. It's very nice of you. Oh, very cool. Well, I'm excited. First off, to introduce you to our people. I was talking about in the video a little bit, but you guys have no idea how much this man has impacted your lives indirectly and it's a lot of you guys directly as well. I'm just my huge honor to have a chance to interview you. I'm not going to lie, I'm kind of nervous, but I'm also excited. And we're going to talk about some of the questions I'm going to ask them about funnels and some of the fatal flaws that a lot of us as internet marketers have when we're focusing on our funnels. But before we got into the funnel stuff, I had a personal question that I wanted to ask just because I know where I got my start and Frank Kern and Ryan Dyson, how some of you will all come back and basically say I got my start from Dan Kennedy, but I'm curious how you got started in this. I don't even know that story. I made it all up. I did it all. I had a brief sojourn in normal traditional advertising and quickly determined that it was 90% bullshit. That the agency agenda and the client agenda were not very well synced. I had an agency owner, very famous, had agency owner say to me, kid, what you want is a dog food company as a client owned by a rich old guy who's married to the third wife half his age and she has five fluffy poodles because all you got to do is put effin poodle in the ad and you'll keep the client for two years because they have no earthly idea whether what we're doing works or not. And it was about that day that I said, you know, I don't really know if I want to be a part of this. You know, there's more honorable ways to make a living. You could be a lawyer or a politician or a hooker. So I began to really pay attention to mail order. David Ogilvy, one of the great old ad guys who you folks might not know but he does. David's famous rant in his office to his staff at one point was then later reproduced in print by David and headlined only the direct response people really know what they're doing. And direct response came from mail order and direct marketing came from mail order. And so I studied as I got serious about it, the masters of mail order. I got to know many of them. Joe Cosman, Jerry Buchanan, all the way to more contemporary folks like Gary Albert who people might know. And then I began to carve out a niche for myself of applying direct marketing to non-direct marketing businesses. Initially by the way in a niche, chiropractic practices. At the time there were 33,000 chiropractic practices in the country and I had 14,600 I would go through my seminars. We had pretty good market penetration. And by the way that's pre-internet. You had to actually send direct mail and stuff to get them in the room. So I began to carve out this niche of mail order, we didn't use that term, for non-mail order businesses because really the principles, right, so principles should govern strategies and strategies should govern tactics and tools. And to your point, the tools change, they've changed a lot. You know, I'm old. And so they've changed a lot but they don't really change much of anything. Tactics have changed but they don't really change much of anything. And almost no media really goes away. It just gets integrated with the additional media that comes along that we can use if we're intelligent about it. So you know, Yellow Pages before really the websites were a thing, we were doing free recorded messages. And we were really the only people teaching free recorded messages for Yellow Page ads. Joe Polish got it from me. Joe got pretty much every... No, that's not true. Not true, not true. You know, I don't need the phone call. But so we were doing free recorded messages. So the ad was an ad for the ad that now had no length limit to it because as long as I can hold your attention I can keep talking to you. Well now you put the website in instead of the recorded message, although by the way recorded messages still work, because the Yellow Pages ad is an ad for the ad and in some niches the Yellow Pages shock to most of you. If you don't know what it is you can Google it. They actually still exist. They are alive. They are well. They drive an enormous amount of business in a number of niche categories. Radio was thought AM Radio was going to go away. It didn't. But it is now integrated with online media to the Piot of Client several years ago that are very successful radio ad. The response device was text me your email address and we'll send you my options, trading secret with which I made six bazillion dollars in two days in the pits. And that was the only response device. So the direct marketing principles. And then on the more on sort of the success side of things which you are personally fascinated with I know sort of the history of personal development and success philosophy. Magnetism is also a real thing. And discussions of it go back to the turn of the century. So I began to use the systems of direct marketing to create a magnetism a pole rather than a push. What Seth Godin later called permission. So that your ideal prospects are chasing you rather than you chasing them. Right. At the time in the real estate business it was called farming setting up a prospect base typically geographic but it doesn't have to be and then nurturing it and being there all the time and so forth. We taught that to chiropractors. We built very successful practices with it. So that's sort of the history. So back then do you call it back back then or is that something in a newer term you started using actually the first magnetic marketing system product was called the small business emergency survival system because it was birthed during the Jimmy Carter presidency which again you can Google it. And the Jimmy Carter presidency the Jimmy Carter recession was really crushing to small business much the same way to the same extent that the recent virus lockdowns and stay at homes and other disruption has been to small business. That recession you had double digit interest rates has the base rate. I mean mortgages if you had good credit and 20 percent down had carried 18 percent interest. You had double digit interest rates. You had double digit unemployment. You had double digit inflation. I mean they're arguing now is it 2 percent 6 percent. I laugh when I hear it. I'm going I'm you know my businesses will be gone when it was 16 percent. I mean if you turned around the another price sticker was on the loaf of bread on top of yesterday's price sticker which was on top of the price sticker from the day before. And so the market they weren't thinking about marketing. They were thinking about survival. And one of the things any good copywriter knows is the Robert Collier principle which is enter the conversation already that they're already having in their mind. What if it's huge if it's consumers. What are they talking about at the kitchen table in the dead of night. If it's business owners what are they talking about with themselves as they drive home at the end of the day. And nobody was really talking about advertising or marketing. They were having a how do we survive this conversation. Has that cleared the product ever changed. The cover came off and the magnetic marketing system cover went on. And the magnetic so the magnetic concept was there. I just didn't use that that language. Interesting. Another side question had no one can go into the core questions but I just show I don't care. All right. As long as we want. Well I remember so one of the first things when I came into your world when the first things I learned initially was was media. I was I ran an internet. I told people I run internet business and you're like no you run a business and you happen to use the internet as a channel. That was one big thing. But the second thing was I wanted to be a public speaker. I'd seen some people speak on stage and sell. I'm going to do that. I remember going to that's how that's all I got there. I saw Zade when I was 15. Oh really when you're 15. That's crazy. And I stood at the back of the room and I counted the money. I think I was like all the people around the back like see how much they made. But I remember for me like I had gotten invited once twice to speak and I spoke and I bombed every single time because I thought that for me to be successful I was going to come and I was going to teach and blow their minds and they were going to run the back and buy. And after I bombed that's when I came into your world and one of the very first things I ended up buying at the first super conference I went to was your public speaking CD course which was like this fat. And I remember going home listening to all the CDs and it was and I started hearing all of the stories that you guys had about when you were speaking at the big the big events and all those kind of things and and and then learning how to actually speak to sell as opposed to just speak to entertainer to educate or things like that. But when did that part come in me started going and actually speaking at those big conferences big events and selling your. I did a lot of crappy small rooms first. Yeah. You know there's a episode of Louis C.K.'s comedy show with my friend Joan Rivers in it and Louis is in the stinking side lounge of the of the Trump casino as a matter of fact and Jones in the main room and Louis is just quit because they won't let him do Trump jokes and Jones says to him are you insane. Nobody in our business quits you know quit. It's too hard to get. He says yeah but it's easy for you to say you're in the big main room. I'm over here in the lounge with the six drunks and the two people in the quarter breeding and you know I'm not I'm not really giving up that much. Right. And Jones says I was there six months ago and I'll probably be there again before it's over. The way you get to the big room is you do the small room. The big events that that you I think refer to that I did for nine years. The only constants on that were Ziggadye. There were other speakers and we were mostly in basketball arenas for seven of the nine years. Audiences of 15 to our biggest audience was 35,600 people or something at the start of the day. Now by the time I got them there was not 35,000 people there. I followed the last famous person. But that tour started with its owner, got my name a Peter Lowe, Zigg, me and a memory expert doing half days with if we were lucky a thousand people there. I mean I remember we were in the Akron Civic Theater and literally there were rats right around the DeGreed Road backstage. You know and I said gee this isn't good. And Zigg said you'll get over it. Well I saw him speak at an Amway rally when I was 15. And I had already begun to understand that very often shovel sellers do better financially than shovel users. It dates all the way back to the Gold Rush, right? And what he did that day different than what he did with a general audience was first of all you'd have to understand the multi-level culture but it was brilliant. I mean brilliant. Extremely manipulative. Well I mean look the principle is this. People love to buy under certain circumstances. But they still rarely do it without being guided to the purchase. I've known two speakers in my entire life who would use your most speakers when they start and all make the same mistake. They all think they're going to come in and they're going to dazzle them with their content and then sort of at the last minute oh by the way I got stuff. And they think everybody is going to go run for the stuff because they were so dazzling. Well it actually almost works in the opposite direction. So I've only known two in my entire life who could get away with it. Everybody else from word one it has to be engineered to drive the sale. And if you have a favorite joke that does not move it you can't tell that joke. You can do that like here after you've organized a giant tribe and you get to speak to them at times where you don't want to sell anything. Now you can indulge yourself and they will indulge you. When you are speaking to sell in person or when you're building a webinar or you're doing anything one to many teleconference they're making a difference. Teleseminar still work by the way. There's this thing called a phone. There's no visible porno on it. So sometimes the concentration levels higher. Seriously. I have a client who split tests every three months. Webinar, teleseminar, same script and he still is outperforming with his teleseminar and he is with the webinar. So the whole thing has to be engineered to create the sale. And speaking just to get a standing ovation that's actually relatively easy. That's a matter of pandering. You can do it if you just know a few things about the audience. Tommy Hopkins used to say we would ride from the airport to the hotel in the arena off into the same city and he would always quiz the cab driver. He said because I need to know about sports in this town this week and I don't feel like reading the sports page and I need to know the town's claim to fame. Because if I use that in the first five minutes when I'm up there I buy some quick rapport. So you can do that kind of stuff and you get a standing ovation. Getting credit cards is much tougher. Much tougher. I built a speak to sell seminar for Joan Revers and Joan had never done it. She had done QVC, home shopping and a star at that. But she had never come up here on a stage with 300 people in a hotel room and done a presentation and pitched stuff and driven them to the back of the room to buy. I'm not even sure she'd ever seen it. But she certainly had never done it. But Joan is a tough customer so she pretty much undid everything I wanted her to do. And then she did it and we came back from the first break and she said how did we do? I said well first of all there's no we in this program here. I said I did fine but you stunk up the place. I said you got 380 people here on Long Island who waited for four hours to get in here to see you and you sold two units because you didn't do the pitch which was 16 minutes long. Now to her credit she said something to me I won't repeat. And then she went up on stage and got everybody back and she said I have to apologize to all of you. I really screwed this up. I didn't really explain this system that you all need to own for yourselves and your families. So I'm going to explain it to you now and then we're going to take another break. And she pitched and she killed. And then the next night because we were testing it in four cities she killed from the beginning. But it's a common error you know you just think people are going to buy and they will but usually not without help. Yeah I remember hearing you in the CDs say that like you knew in your script like exactly this is where I can drink my water this is where I left this is the joke. I remember thinking like that'll never happen to me. And after I got my webinar that worked to launch click funnels I did it a hundred times in a row back to back to back. I knew it was the same way I knew where my joke was when I was drinking the water like everything in it was just flawless and so. The AV crew at the big events could set their watch by when I was going to say a sentence in 75 minutes because I was never off. When I started speaking to sell in 1977 the founder of the National Speakers Association got by name of Kavett Robert interesting guy but we don't have time but but Kavett was a what we then called a platform sales person. Right he only spoke to sell didn't care if he got a fee or not he was you know. And Kavett said to me it's infinitely easier to get a new qualified audience than it is to get a new speech that works. Now when I heard it I thought with the arrogance of youth and Kavett 70 some odd years old at the time I thought that's the laziest thing I have ever heard in my life. Ten years later I thought that's one of the smartest I've ever heard in my life because it's absolutely you know when you get a winner regardless of the media which if you're the media fine I mean I worked in the infomercial industry a lot for 15 years when you get a show that works you want to buy all the time you can. You don't want to be working on a new show you want to ride get that one out of the barn every day and get it to the track and in as many different media as you possibly can. So like Kavett said he's a great guy. And in as many different media as you possibly can. So like Beachbody some of you probably pay attention to Beachbody. Carl Deichler goes way back to Mike Main years at Guthrie Wanker and Direct Mail guy Craig Simpson does Beachbody's Direct Mail and you know Craig. You can take most of the successful Beachbody infomercials or even their two minute spots and you can turn them into a direct mail piece that works and why wouldn't you. Right I mean because at some point that message isn't going to work anymore and you're going to have to replace it but as long as it's working you want to deploy it in every way that you can that at least breaks even if you have a back end if you are in the business of acquiring customers. Yeah that actually interesting enough leads to our first real question. So I mean so much fun I can do this forever. So let's shift a little bit to that then. So we wanted to talk when we were kind of planning this out we talked about like in our world you guys are all funnel hackers we build cells funnels that's where our focal points at but there's a lot of things that in fact we title this presentation five fatal flaws of funnels and so there's some questions I'm asking specifically for you guys to start looking at your funnels differently looking at your business differently that I think are these core principles that had such a big impact on me that I learned directly from you. And so the first one this one was interesting for me because when I first started my business I built a funnel I started driving people to it I started making sales and I was making money but I remember one day probably a year or two into the business waking up and laying in bed and being like so miserable because I hated my customers and I remember sitting there thinking I wish that I had a boss they could fire me because I don't want to go in and have to do my customer support and talk to my I just have the wrong customers. I think a lot of times because it's so easy nowadays to build a funnel and start getting traffic we build this amazing funnel but then we get the wrong prospects wrong people in. I'd love for you to talk about about making sure we're getting the right people so we have businesses that. Well and by the way it also happens a lot when people are only using cheap media because they say to themselves what differences it make right but at some point in time if you're good at scale you run out in your cheap media you actually now have to start buying media and now it matters a lot right so a key to being magnetic so all sorts of things help right in being magnetic with marketing but so people will often ask me for the pie chart which is success with a direct marketing campaign what percentage of it is due to this what percentage of it is due to that what percentage of it is and of course there is no such pie chart because it varies but more than half of scalable sustainable success in direct marketing and I used to care about saying this when a million dollars or so a year of my income came from writing copy now I don't care so completely unfettered truth but more than half has to do with the audience that you've selected the potential customer client patient or donor that you've selected and if you selected really well mediocre marketing will often get extraordinary results if you've selected poorly exceptional marketing will very often get mediocre results and over a long term will turn out to be a economic failure because the customer value over time is not very long or it's not very high or the dynamic that you've described happens is the business's leadership winds up with a bunch of customers they don't like and then naturally they don't treat them very well and now their value goes down right so the most important principle about magnetic marketing is what I've always described as message to market match and the tighter that match is you know John Carlton's story about the the ad campaign for the one-legged left-handed golfer right well if you can get that in front of a one-legged left-handed golfer see that's a slam dunk so actually the smart thing is to find a a hungry underserved specific affordably reachable market and then build a message to match it and you create slam dunks for yourself so relevance is important is this message really relevant to this person appropriate is the message really appropriate to these people is the message understandable easily recognized oh okay I get it right like this message gee I now understand I want to do all this complicated stuff and I can't do it with three by five cards and colored stickers and eight different computer programs that don't talk to each other and etc I need quick funnels right well that's you kind of get that if you're doing any marketing at all now then you get to make a decision about well it's quick funnels the right one or the wrong one but you get the concept right so the concept has to be understandable usually there has to be affinity whoever is delivering the message has to be um um as they have some connection to the audience we had a speaker on the big tour his name was Ted Brower a nutrition speaker and Ted and that's not an easy topic to do in a big open public rally and all that Ted did the 10 foods never to eat and then he sold a package and a coaching program from the stage and the first time we did Birmingham Alabama uh Ted nearly zeroed and he was mystified because same speech same energy level I did the same dance before I went out there to get myself jazzed up everything's the same and I zeroed how can this be I said Ted you're in Birmingham Alabama you just told them they can't drink Dr Pepper they can't eat moon pies and they can't eat catfish I said that's the whole diet what what exactly do you expect these people to eat if they can't buy it at the convenience store they eat eat it but I mean you got it you you got to modify that message a little bit to match the fact that we're in Birmingham Alabama or don't work Birmingham if your integrity will not let you do so message to market match is what is where we find magnetism so like I've always understood their audiences that are no good for me okay and I'm no good for them either all right we corporate okay we are not you know gonna sink all right and there's no point in me trying to make that happen because there's a lot of people with whom I'm like the perfect match so why not find them right so that's like the key to this is sort of source where are they coming from and who are they not who are you so most people start with I got this stuff I want to sell I'm I've written a book which I spent six years on you know about poetry or gender studies or you know whatever and now I want to go now I want to go in search of somebody who will buy it which is problem-riddled right and often that no matter what you do you can't make that work now this also speaks to precision I'll probably come back to it but like there's almost no business that'll work off of one funnel there or shouldn't there's almost no business that'll work off of one ad campaign there's almost no because there's subsets of every audience in every market and the tighter the match you know the better so like if you have orthodontists who run radio ads about Invisalign and a radio ad drives them to the orthodontist website but that's a generic website about everything the orthodontist does the person who went there thinking Invisalign Invisalign Invisalign is instantly disappointed oh crap it's just another orthodontist and off they go the orthodontist has got if he wants to dentures he's got to have a complete marketing system for dentures a complete marketing system for Invisalign and never the twain shall meet right if he's marketing to well you've probably read it a bunch of people won't have one of the 10 best marketing books ever written is called the Purpose Driven Church yeah you've read this oh well you learn something every day yes make a note because you'll be excited particularly the chapter that is not titled this but is very predatory so Purpose Driven Church is written by Rick Warren pastor Rick Warren his best seller is Purpose Driven Life so Purpose Driven Church is for pastors and it's really a marketing book for pastors but it never uses that term because they get cringy about it I have a friend and a client who was with Rick Warren for a number of years his name is Nelson Cersey and he owns a company called Church Leader Insights and he has a coaching program 3,600 pastors in it right now and I always talk to Nelson about the church business and he constantly asked me don't call it that well you got a lead generation you got right what what what what what is it if it's not you know a business but the Purpose Driven Church Rick Warren's book there's a chapter in it about susceptibility right which sometimes a direct marketing we call probability okay and so like at the grassroots level he has a list of 10 susceptibility categories that you as a pastor may want to build a complete marketing system for and do target marketing for so for example you can go down in your town to the police station and you can get a list every Monday you can get the arrest records and look at them and you can see who had a domestic violence or a juvenile delinquency related police call to their house they are substantially more susceptible to a come to church message than their neighbor who doesn't have any problems now tested you can't show up and say we went down the police station we know you you don't do that you just show up at the right time with your best marketing message right so it's a really interesting marketing book I I commend that's cool very cool give her a round of applause for that that actually reminds me of one of the stories I think you wrote about it I don't know originally the first time I read it was in the the ultimate sales letter I think you talked the story about the welcome guest versus annoying pest would you really tell that story because I think it kind of ties into that and the marketing messages that people are yeah so people show up has annoying pests precisely because they don't get message to market match going in right so this is actually true I was living in Phoenix I was home I stayed home I never went to the office and I didn't want to be there they didn't want me to be there you kind of know this gig right so I worked at home and it was a weekday middle of the day and we had big wood double doors and there's this monstrous banging on the wood double doors ringing the doorbell and then banging on the doors and this was a recession so the Avon ladies and the Jehovah Witnesses were carpooling and I told that joke for 35 years I've only ever got one complaint and it was not from the Jehovah Witness it was it was from an Avon sales manager who I had just destroyed or sales force and self-esteem and they'll never work again you know just completely humorless today you'd of course be banned from Facebook but so because I know no one invited is coming over I know this is an annoying pest and I'm busy I think I was on the phone so I ignore it confident that if you ignore a pest generally it will go away right it will tire of trying to get you to pay attention and move on to the next house so this goes on for a while but I'm pretty stubborn so I can ignore I can ignore a lot and next thing I know this person has climbed over the back wall of my property which by the way has shards of glass on top of the to discourage this idea and they are there they've in the backyard they've surpassed the pool and the hot tub and stuff and they are now banging on the sliding glass door which they can see me through so now you're kind of a you know if you don't so okay so the message is all of the shrubbery in your front yard is on fire and the flames are licking at the at the stuff and of course not everybody was walking out of the cell phone then so his message was you call the fire department and I'll go back out there and work the hose now at that second in time he went from being a most annoying pest to a most welcome guest and and the only thing that changed was right message right time right that's it so you know in our world there's been a what I don't know a decade long insanity of email delivery drops open rates go down so marketer who was sending one a day since two then his open rates go down further so he sends four then his open rates go down further so he sends eight until his open rate is zero and he's sending one every five minutes right you can't really amp up being an annoying pest and get results it's hard work a super salesman could do it you know like I've a friend do I always say you could give him a crate to stand on and put him in the bus station and he will walk out of there having sold five thousand dollar coaching programs to every vagrant who's in the place but he's a pretty rare fellow right most people are not you know they don't have the combination of that skill I mean I say I could get a check out of a rock I'm not capable but I don't want to I want people to want to give me checks right and that's about this dynamic change of always asking yourself is everything I'm doing here organized so that I'm showing up as a welcome guest that's why we use FedEx a lot instead of direct mail because interest level automatically goes up because it's FedEx package yeah very cool the next question I have for you and it's funny because I quoted you in all my books saying this and then I think three times in the last two days I've quoted you this is like my the quote that the probably the biggest impact we actually had a member who made what would Dan do bracelets yeah which I was really uncomfortable with having these handed out I didn't stop them but I was on that that's awesome the quote is whoever can spend the most money to acquire customer wins and especially in our world right now I don't know how much you follow like the recent like Facebook and Apple fights like cost to acquire customers has gone up across the internet and I think that it's headed towards like real acquisition cost yeah so I love but I love because I think people in this room are good at making good fun a lot of times the economics don't don't work at the end of the day I love you talk a little more about that yeah so so math is the gravity of the direct marketing business right and outright defined gravity is just not an intelligent thing to do my during my rich family stage I had a very big bedroom on the second story of the house and I had my own balcony which really was the carport roof over the back driveway and I got a superman Halloween costume and I was young enough to think the secret was the cape and so I took a running start and flew ever so briefly um and and I tried it a second time in case I had screwed it up somehow but um but it turns out gravity kind of you know is a real thing and so marketing is about behavioral psych and math that's what it's about and most people love the behavioral psych part and they hate the math part and they were often trying to overcome bad math with some compensating idea right I've got a product that the whole world needs I've got a product that these people need I've got the best doodad ever invented I've got the best copywriter that's ever walked the face of the earth etc etc etc nothing overcomes bad economics nothing ever and if you're a copywriter and somebody tries to tempt you into this which happened to me once every three or four years you should resist the temptation if you don't want to screw up your scorecard because you are not going to win it's like the graveyard of marketing is bad economics and if you can temporarily live with bad math because you're only using cheap media it's only a matter of time before you'll be in trouble so you want to kind of get that right from the beginning beyond that so to your quotation it has to do with cost per lead and it has to do with cost per sale first of all in any competitive environment whoever figures out how to spend the most for their lead and their sale wins they buy speed they buy scale they buy discouragement of competition and they buy sustainability now you got to figure that out without it putting you in a bankruptcy court if you're not like uber where you can go 12 years before you make a profit you've got to figure out a little shorter runway than that so this is why and as acquisition cost goes up which it does all the time see there's always been cheap media early always when I started working in the infomercial business tv station still didn't understand what we were doing in a local market like in phoenix you could buy all the half hours you wanted from midnight until eight in the morning for like 200 bucks a half hour they actually turned the tv off like after jody carson and the tom steider show on nbc they played the national anthem and there was nothing on the tv until eight o'clock the next morning so when we walked in and said we'll buy that dead time the station guy says what do you give us for it how about 200 bucks station guy says to himself these people are crazy right will you agree to do it for six weeks sure great here here's the keys to the building you come in flip the switch you know well you could have some pretty crappy marketing and you could win but with the first transaction that's when you saw a lot of seminar moved to tv kind of infomercials you saw the dave del dottos of the world we just took them out of the free preview room we stuck them in a tv studio with a rented audience and a camera and a light and we let him do his thing right because if you saw one course you were home free right well five years later that half hour is 20 000 dollars well now you got to get good right now you got to like figure out a business you got to you you got to be able to mitigate that cost you got to you can't miss any opportunity for money right but if you do if you do now you win big because like everybody else kind of surrenders so in tv for example my client got the rancor i work on proactive the acne go up from show number one until the last show and um and wrote copy for everything but tv because at peak we're using 22 different media including yellow pages which is an interesting side story in and of itself um and um there's no magic to that right there's only three fda approved ingredients you could make an acting claim for so every glop has one two or three of those has its chief active ingredients and then the other stuff varies a little bit but candidly glop is pretty much glop some of it's a little better than others some of it's a little worse than others glop is glop same thing ladies with skincare products i know you don't like to hear it it's just like house paint there's i'm serious there's four levels of it and that's all there is there's the level made for walmart and the cvs store and there's the level made for the departments and 90 percent of it's the same glop um so there was no magic to the glop there's no magic to the show if you sat there with a pad a pen and a stopwatch all the secrets of the show are visible just like when i saw zig speak when i was 15 i said if i just get a tape of this i like i got the blueprint right and then who else is doing this tom opkins great who else is doing this brian tracy great so if i go get tapes from of all three of them pitching and i transcribe them i got the blueprint well same thing with infomercials there's not that much variable capability so if you so you could duplicate the show right which you couldn't necessarily duplicate which is why there never was a serious competitor in all the years that proactive was built to a company that sold for a billion dollars uh to nestling who is busily destroying it um um they couldn't wait um um well it happens all the time you know well the entrepreneur got it this far the moron now will bring in professional management to imagine what we can do to it yeah i afghanistan that's um um i mean seriously could your dog have screwed my dog would not have screwed it up as bad you know dog would have gone civilians last so um so we never had a serious competitor why because they couldn't figure out the economics how the hell do we spend an ever-increasing amount to acquire a customer so for thirty nine dollars we were spending three hundred then we were spending six hundred then you were spending nine hundred well people would duplicate the show run it for a weekend see the numbers and commit suicide right you don't have to out compete them so the first truth about all this is there really is no bad number once you get a number and you know what it is you're challenged now is how do i bridge the gap to a number i could afford and then go out and acquire all the good customers i want if i could figure out that far no i was on the air for nine years with a little show called go by the inch it sold a business opportunity where you got these spools of gold stuff and you went out to the swap meet you we were on the air for nine consecutive years non-stop every day every night built a fabulous business with a really cheap show and um i i don't know five six seven eight attempts right at copycating it but they didn't copycat the math so for example this cost we had to add a phone room we didn't have a phone room to start with we generated leads we sent out packages orders came in right beautiful now the cost of acquisition goes up oh crap we can't afford to waste these leads right now he didn't even have a follow-up sequence i said like we can't afford to waste these leads anymore we got to have a 12 step follow-up sequence okay now we got that now the cost of acquisition is still going up it's the line you can't you're not going to be able to stay on the air unless we got telemarketers calling the non-responsive schmucks who won't respond to the 16 steps right so now we got a phone room with all the ugliness that comes with it right every car in the parking lot with the closed pole in the backseat with the clothes hanging on it people on the brakes out smoking you know everything that comes with having a phone room then the cost is still going up so now we're collaborating we're lead swapping with other opportunity marketers hey you're generating leads we're generating leads they're saying no to our thing that doesn't mean they're done looking for opportunity just means they don't like our thing we'll swap you our leads after we've used them for a month and you give us your leads after you've used them for a month cuts each of our costs in half right by the time i was done we were sharing our leads with nine other marketers in order to stay on the air and consequently we could spend more money than any other opportunity marketer ever figured out how to do right because they kept working on a creative oh we need a better show you know i did a lot of work for weight watchers at one point that i was in their phone room for a at the time they were handling one million inbound calls a year all 80 fat women 20 fat guys who wanted to lose weight the conversations were all the same right i got a wedding coming up beach vacation you know i got it i got it dropped away or post divorce three months after the divorce by the way that's the susceptibility mark for cosmetic surgery cosmetic dentistry it's a big marker the three to six month number which goes all the way back to rick warren and by the way you can get that information um uh you know women buy five times at five times the rate skincare products uh by direct by direct marketing if you arrive the month of their birthday during the year as opposed to any other month same marketing you don't have to change anything you just have to be there in the birthday month because they look into mere more they're you know obsessing more which you know guys don't pretty much we don't especially for wear he gets like really i'm looking to see if i got more bags under my eyes but she is and and god bless her by the way but um um and and by the way that doubles again in benchmark birthdays oh like 40 50 yeah yeah yeah yeah yeah yeah so so there's these these there's these weird little susceptibility factors you know to everything that you can that you can manipulate uh if you know them but the math is really important so like the kind of the rule of thumb for direct marketing is you need an eight to one mark and if you got less than that you're starting out in trouble you're not going to be able to use all the media that's available to you once you have a winner you're going to be handicapped right and if you can't get eight to one but you're close then you got to start figuring out this ancillary and back end stuff of mitigating these costs so that you are always the one with the spend power in the marketplace now this is the exact opposite of what most marketers do so most marketers first of all try and spend as little as humanly possible to get a customer all those guarantees they get crappy customers and they don't get enough of them then if they've fooled around with let's say eight media they take the two that are cheapest they'll never use the three over here that are the most expensive they never dollar cost average of them together you know i mean if you got a website that works for like stop smoking lose weight every door direct mail will work for you the economics aren't exactly the same but close and close a val pack will work for you um you can google it it's a thing um still exists um so you can have the best funnel in the world presenting the best product but if you got bad math and you don't do something about that math uh you may be okay in the early going but you're going to get bit yeah very cool all happiest in the basement writing i never wanted any part of this i stuttered uncontrollably all the way through my teens but it turns out that building an institutional business a nameless faceless uh devoid of personality business is much harder uh it requires a lot more capital and it takes a lot more time because people like to do business with people that they know like admire respect trust and feel affinity to and you can't do that when it's x y z company right and the proof is even big corporations when they get in trouble they trot out a human right even if they weren't doing it before so kind of pretty happy pretty happy sleepy beauty's castle playboy mansion it kind of depends on who you are but um um the the playboy mansion toast let me see if i could do it it was written by bob facebook or instagram or pinterest or anybody else uh because you'll get at them less and less and less and less under more and more and more restrictions over time you got to move your customers to your own media platform now i would i would say world i would use the word world and so stan lee did that brilliantly um um he's the first guy ever there was a a rule in comic books that you never let the reader you never acknowledged to the reader that any of this wasn't real right and stan broke that rule to smithereens and let everybody in on the gag and the fans not only knew the characters they knew the artists they knew the writers there was a column in almost every marvel comic book called uh stands bullpen where they talked about uh what jack Kirby was doing on his vacation and what the next storyline was going to be and it was all kind of written tongue in cheek teen kaita lingo uh but we were in the marvel world uh kids badgered their parents to take them to the marvel offices office with his drawing board in it and so forth they've kept that a lot all these years so so what all three of those guys did just brilliantly is they took their customers they organized them as a group and they put them in a world of their own with media they owned and controlled now that didn't stop them from using every other media available to them but they were very cognizant of the fact that that um so there's a there's a money principle um wealth uh security and freedom mostly the three things we all want none of them come from what we do because what we do is always inherently out front you can't buy an asset that depreciates faster and look i own resources they eat while i sleep but they ain't as dumb as those things oh i mean to each their own by the way but it's fine uh i'm just saying lifestyle escalates with income so you know god makes 50 000 his first year he thinks he's done something i used to think jesus you made a million dollars a year i mean you were like customers with whom i had equity she had cash cash disappears equity in customers doesn't uh the first thing i heard for a bunch of customers because we there's a principle about this i call sending whom you have good relationship and you have all their data and so forth then so i got as we announced the divorce um i started to get faxes and phone messages uh when are you sending out the invoice don't do that well the plug a book um i have a book about this called almost alchemy more from fewer and less and what you can't afford um either because of rising costs of acquisition or because you want maximum power in the marketplace or both is you can't afford to accidentally or unknowingly leave money behind now if you make a decision knowingly on purpose that yeah there's money over there to get but for the following five good reasons we're going to ignore it fine but if you don't know it um and you're accidentally leaving money behind then if nothing else befalls you and there's all sorts of bad things that could happen as a result of but if nothing else befalls you some competitor who will identify those sources of money and not leave them behind will catch you kick your butt and go right past you steve jobs warning was right now there's two kids in a garage trying to figure out how to kill apple and there'll be a day when two kids will and that's that's like healthy paranoia right which is an oxymoron but it's a good strategy so there's just a ton of money left in like in like there's like so I'll give you an example um the strategy is called appointment no sale okay and so what happens when you do a sales presentation however it is you do it and uh some percentage buy some number buy and some percentage or some number don't you know 90% of businesses are god he books appointments with he'll tell you two thirds it's really one third and two thirds of them escape now one third of them are worthless but one third of them aren't right so what do you do with that third right he'll say well uh if he's honest he'll say nothing or he'll say the next time we're doing a seminar we market to him again well that's stupid they were already there right or if bertha has time we ever call them well bertha never has time right because bertha don't want to call them that's their that's their system all right it's beyond you know um i had a client years ago would say nothing they just saw my best webinar they're not going to buy from that scrum i'm going well there's going to be a day when you're not going to like that answer and you're going to want because it was a direct order show it was the lead generation show and so you called the phone room and the telemarketers talked two thirds of the people out of buying that's what they did well when you do tv you surge so your phone room is full of people as ronnie grand says uh they're they're in the telemarketing center taking your calls because they were too dumb to work at pop ice chicken that's what you got so they would talk two thirds of the buyers out of buying right uh so we sent a letter from fran talk to the new who is the host of that and we sent an audio cassette now this is how this is 87 of the infomercial with some added testimonials on it and the same offer we converted a significant number positive i learned from that and when i spoke on a big seminar tour took me two years because i was dumb and lazy but i got it by the third year we would take everybody that was there who didn't buy my stuff and we would send them a big long sales letter the next day which really was a transcription of my entire speech with a different opening paragraph and a different ending paragraph in an order form and we never failed to make a profit not not big not big but when the customer's back end value is in the tens of thousands it's big in future money not necessarily present bank but future bank and we're always managing both banks if we're doing a good job with our businesses so there's a version of appointment no sale in every business and most people leave the money behind and they could be using that money to pay more for leads to pay more to make a sale or they could be using that money for wine women and song if they insist on it uh or they could be using that money to invest in untouchables outside of their own business there are all sorts of things you could use the money for why leave it lay there now you're gonna like you're gonna want it to pay your taxes i mean so that's a good reason to get it well there's all these leaks internally in a business and externally in a business typical chiropractic office they let the front desk staff go to lunch together and put the phones on voicemail i'm serious this is like you know afghanistan now half of you giggled when you heard it right you go no no you don't want voicemail in the middle of the day when you have three staff people you're paying no you take turns going to lunch so there's always somebody right to take that's the bare minimum in a chiropractic or dental office a long time client of mine j guyer has all the stats if you just if this is all you change you get the phones answered you get the phones answered by a human and you get them answered by a human who doesn't mind doing it in part because you've incentivized them well you can't have birth of the bookkeeper taking new patient calls because you know she's pissed off because she was interrupted and on and on so those three things but if that's all you do you increase gross revenue by 20 percent that's how much is being lost just by that so if you run around a business and you find five of those you double the business if you can find five internal and five external you double double that's cool that's so applicable in our businesses too yeah first time you talk about that I start looking at my sales funnel and like from email sequences to funnel everything and start looking at where all the spots I guess we'll be done so I met I met the national infobrational marketing association convention one year with coffee raker and um it was at a time when normal companies were starting to flirt with the idea of doing infomercials now nobody knew what the hell they but they thought they wanted to do this right and so Greg brings a guy over to introduce to me and he's the vice president of signed Francis Ford Coppola to direct you so I burst out laughing which annoys Greg is a very political you know small p political diplomat kind of person um and I burst out laughing and of course the guy takes umbridge at it and what are you laughing at I said well if you wanted to make godfather five Francis Ford Coppola is the right choice if you want to sell some dishwashers I doubt he's ever sold a dishwasher in his fucking life let me tell you who you want who'll cost you a lot less you want a broken down old door to door vacuum cleaner sales guy and he's got one foot bigger than the other one because he shoves it which opens and he's got a three-piece reversible suit that you buy at JC Petty with holes in it from cigarette burns and he's not perfectly shaven right and he's got a little alcohol on his breath that's the guy you want is your director because he knows how to sell shit right and and he can hire the guys who run the cameras he don't need to know how to run the camera we're all at Joe Sugarman's place in Hawaii one year at Joe Corral's ill-advisedly a Microsoft executive off the beach to come in and talk to everybody and this guy's he's spent a half an hour putting charts up on the wall it is no idea what he's doing we're in a direct marketing seminar and he wanted to see them and I can't remember who was there now but he was from QVC and he got really impatient and he stood up and said whoa whoa whoa whoa I sold like 50 million dollars worth of stuff on QVC last year I don't know how the picture gets from the TV studio to Martha's house I don't know I don't want to know I don't need to know Kennedy doesn't know how to cut down a tree and turn it into paper to do direct mail we don't want to know any of this shit we're here to talk about how to sell stuff we can hire people to cut down trees right they're readily available and that's you know that's the the media mistake that happens with web designers right because they're like graphic artists and they're like you know they what do they know they've never sold anything and we're selling stuff with media that's what we're doing right and and that's important that's awesome this has
